title: Three retries to a dead payments service
tagline: The flash sale browned Payments out at 24% errors. Your retry policy burned it down.
brief: Checkout charges cards through a Payments Service that wraps the provider's synchronous SDK — one connection per in-flight charge, capped at 4 per pod, 8 pods, 80 ms a call. It was sized to brown out gracefully: past ~32 concurrent charges it sheds the overflow and keeps serving. Tonight's flash sale lifts checkout from 60 to 84 RPS — inside its 100 RPS capacity. But the Checkout API's HTTP client carries retryPolicy: maxRetries 3, and there is no circuit breaker anywhere on the path.
symptom: First second of the sale: Payments at 84% CPU, ~24% of charges timing out on the in-flight cap — degraded but alive. One second later the log prints 'retry storm amplifying load 1.3×': the retries of those failures stack onto live traffic, 110 effective RPS against 100 RPS of capacity. CPU pins at 100%, deeper errors feed a bigger multiplier (it peaks near 2.4×, ~200 RPS), and within seconds the node card flips to CRASH and never comes back. Nominal traffic never exceeded 84 RPS.

phases.description: Normal evening checkout — 60 RPS, Payments at 60% utilization
Flash sale goes live — 84 RPS sustained against 100 RPS of Payments capacity

schemaMemory.aiNotes: Write-heavy charge ledger; ~84 RPS against thousands of RPS of Postgres throughput — the database is never the constraint in this design.

apiContracts.description: Charge a card for an order

diagnosis.question: Nominal traffic never exceeded 84 RPS against 100 RPS of Payments capacity, yet Payments hit 100% CPU and crashed seconds into the sale. What's the root cause?

options.text: Checkout's maxRetries: 3 with no circuit breaker turns the 24% brown-out into geometrically amplified load — retries stack onto live traffic, the overload deepens the errors, and the errors deepen the multiplier until Payments dies.
options.explain: Right. With e = last second's error rate, retries multiply forwarded load by 1 + e + e² + e³: at e = 0.24 that's 1.31×, so 84 nominal RPS becomes ~110 effective against 100 RPS of capacity. Now the service is past saturation, errors climb, and the multiplier climbs with them — it peaks near 2.4× (~200 RPS) on its way to the 4× ceiling. With no breaker to fail fast, nothing interrupts the loop before CPU pins above 98% and the node crashes for good.

options.text: Payments is simply undersized for the sale — 84 RPS is more than it can really take, and it would have died retries or not.
options.explain: Capacity is 8 instances × (1000 ÷ 80 ms) = 100 RPS, so 84 RPS is ρ = 0.84 — watch the very first second of the sale: CPU 84%, a 24% in-flight-cap brown-out, and no crash. That is a degraded-but-stable operating point; it only spirals after the log prints 'retry storm amplifying load'. If raw capacity were the root cause, the first sale tick would already have pinned CPU at 100%.

options.text: The Ledger DB saturated under the sale's write burst and backpressured Payments into the ground.
options.explain: The Ledger DB card never leaves ~1% utilization — at most ~84 RPS of charge inserts against 3 000 write / 5 000 read RPS of throughput — and its error rate is zero for the entire run. Payments died of inbound overload from its caller, not of anything below it: this cascade flows top-down from the retrying client, not bottom-up from storage.

options.text: The Checkout API itself saturated during the sale, and its overflow spilled onto Payments.
options.explain: Checkout has 8 × (1000 ÷ 10 ms) = 800 RPS of capacity and never sees more than 84 RPS — its CPU sits near 10% all run with zero errors. Retries don't load the caller in this model; they multiply the caller's OUTBOUND traffic. Checkout stays green the entire time it is machine-gunning its dependency to death — and that healthy-looking caller is exactly why retry storms are hard to spot from the caller's own dashboard.

fix.objective: Break the feedback loop at the wire: keep the Payments Service alive through the whole flash sale — no crash, no 100%-CPU death spiral, and no multi-second p99 from amplified retry waves.

criteria.label: Nothing crashes — Payments survives the sale
The storm never lands: Payments averages under 45% CPU once the sale is on

hints: Payments capacity = 8 × (1000 ÷ 80 ms) = 100 RPS, and the sale is only 84 RPS — yet two seconds in, it's absorbing 110+ RPS. Where did the extra third come from? Find the 'retry storm amplifying load' line in the log.

Every failed charge is retried up to 3 times, so forwarded load multiplies by 1 + e + e² + e³ on the previous second's error rate e. At 24% errors that is 1.31× — already past capacity — and the errors that overload causes push the multiplier toward 4×. The cure is failing FAST when the dependency is sick: click the Checkout API → Payments Service wire and add a circuit breaker.

A breaker with failureThreshold 0.1, failureWindow 1, cooldownSeconds 10, halfOpenTicks 2 trips after the first bad second — before the first retry wave ever lands — then probes roughly every 11 s. Tempering retryPolicy.maxRetries to 1 is good hygiene too, but only the breaker keeps an already-saturated dependency standing.
